1: A shadow: Moses addresses the tribal heads with the law of vows — establishing that a man who makes a vow to YHWH must not break his word (lo yachel devaro). The principle of inviolable speech points toward Christ, whose every word is absolutely reliable: 'heaven and earth will pass away, but my words will not pass away' (Matthew 24:35). Christ is the one person whose vow is utterly unbreakable by nature.
2: A direct type: 'Everything that proceeds out of his mouth he shall do.' The absolute correspondence between word and act in vow-keeping is the standard Christ perfectly embodies. He 'fulfills all righteousness' (Matthew 3:15) — every obligation, every requirement is met in him. His words are deeds; his promises are certainties. 'He who promised is faithful' (Hebrews 10:23).
3: A shadow: A woman in her father's house who makes a vow — the father can annul it if he hears it on the same day. The father's authority over his daughter's vow foreshadows the Father's sovereignty over those under his care. But Christ, as the one who comes from the Father's house, has a vow the Father ratifies — his entire mission is the Father's will: 'I have come down from heaven, not to do my own will but the will of him who sent me' (John 6:38).
4: A theme: If the father hears and holds his peace, all her vows stand. Silence ratifies the vow. The Father's public ratification of Christ at his baptism — 'This is my beloved Son, with whom I am well pleased' (Matthew 3:17) — is the ultimate vow-ratification: the Father's loud endorsement of the Son's entire mission.
5: A theme: But if the father annuls it on the day he hears, YHWH will forgive her because the father annulled it. The protection of the one under covering: her vow that was beyond her ability to keep is cancelled, not held against her. Christ fulfills the obligations his people cannot keep — 'he himself bore our sins in his body' (1 Peter 2:24). The legal obligation that was beyond us is removed because the one over us bore it.
6: A shadow: A woman makes a vow and then marries — the husband hears on the day of marriage and holds his peace, and her vows stand. Marriage ratifies the vow. Paul's image of Christ as husband of the church (Ephesians 5:25-27): Christ the bridegroom takes the church with her imperfect vows, ratifies what he can and absorbs what he cannot. He 'loved the church and gave himself up for her.'
7: A theme: If the husband hears and holds his peace on the day he hears, then her vows stand. The husband's silence is consent; his voice is decision. Christ's silence before Pilate (Matthew 27:14) was not passive — it was purposeful surrender: he did not defend himself because the cup was his to drink. His silence was the loudest possible act of will.
8: A shadow: If the husband annuls the vow on the day he hears, he has made void the obligation incurred by her lips, and YHWH will forgive her. The husband bears the cost of annulment — the wife goes free, but the vow's weight transfers to him. Christ bears what his church could not: 'the LORD has laid on him the iniquity of us all' (Isaiah 53:6). The church is freed; Christ absorbs the cost.
9: A direct type: The vow of a widow or a divorced woman stands — she is not under a man's authority and bears her vow directly before YHWH. The widow stands unmediated before God: her word is her bond, and YHWH receives it directly. The church after Christ's death and before his return is in an analogous position: between the crucifixion and the consummation, it stands directly before the Father through the Spirit (Romans 8:26-27).
10: A theme: A vow made while still in her husband's house binds her. The prior commitments made while under covering remain. The church's promises made in union with Christ bind her to the standard of that union: 'do you not know that your bodies are members of Christ?' (1 Corinthians 6:15). What is done in union with Christ is done in the presence of the absolute standard.
11: A shadow: If the husband heard and held his peace on the day she vowed and the days passed — the silence of sustained days ratifies the vow. The patience of the covenant-keeper who neither annuls nor abandons. Christ's patience with the church across the centuries: 'The Lord is not slow to fulfill his promise as some count slowness, but is patient toward you, not wishing that any should perish' (2 Peter 3:9).
12: A theme: If the husband annuls after silence, he bears her iniquity. The annulment after the fact — reneging — creates guilt in the one who renged. Christ does not renege. 'Having loved his own who were in the world, he loved them to the end' (John 13:1). The surety who makes a promise and keeps it through every pressure is the anti-type of the husband who annulled after silence.
13: A theme: Every vow and every binding oath to afflict oneself — the husband can establish or annul. The power of self-denial vows (fasting, Nazirite dedication) under covenant authority. Christ's own self-denial — 'not my will but yours' (Luke 22:42) — is the ultimate self-affliction vow ratified by the Father and accomplished completely.
14: A shadow: If the husband holds his peace from day to day, he confirms all her vows — by saying nothing he establishes them all. The silent ratification over extended time. God's patience across Old Testament centuries — holding peace through Israel's ups and downs, confirming the covenant through sustained silence before the appointed hour — until 'when the fullness of time had come, God sent forth his Son' (Galatians 4:4).
15: A theme: If he annuls them after he has heard, then he shall bear her iniquity. The covenant head who fails to bear what was his to bear incurs guilt. Christ, the covenant head who bears all that was ours, never incurs this guilt — because he holds nothing back from bearing. 'He who did not spare his own Son but gave him up for us all, how will he not also with him graciously give us all things?' (Romans 8:32).
16: A shadow: These are the statutes that YHWH commanded Moses, between a man and his wife, and between a father and his daughter while still in her youth in her father's house. The covenant architecture of vows — the web of ratification, bearing, and accountability — is the Old Testament form of what Paul describes as the Christ-church marriage: covenant obligations with a head who bears what the other cannot. 'The husband is the head of the wife even as Christ is the head of the church' (Ephesians 5:23).
